title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a transport planner called Bella Shaw talking about the evolution of cycling in cities. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a transport planner called Bella Shaw talking about the evolution of cycling in cities. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Bella Shaw discusses the infrastructure and social changes needed to promote cycling in urban areas.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. I’m Bella Shaw. Ten years ago, if you cycled to work in this city, people thought you were either very brave or very eccentric. Today, it’s the fastest-growing mode of transport. My own interest in urban planning wasn't something I studied initially; it actually began when I participated in a [7]charity bike ride[/7] across Europe. Seeing how cities like Amsterdam and Copenhagen were designed around people rather than cars was a revelation. I realized that our cities didn't have to be loud and polluted.

When we look at the history of the bicycle, it’s always been a tool for social change. In the late 19th century, it played a huge role in the women's suffrage movement, providing a new level of freedom. However, in the mid-20th century, the [8]rise of car ownership[/8] pushed cyclists to the edges of the road. Planning focused entirely on speed and efficiency for motor vehicles, and the bicycle was seen as a relic of the past. We are now working hard to reverse those decades of car-centric design.

The biggest challenge I face is infrastructure. It’s not enough to just paint a white line on the road. For people to feel safe, we need 'segregated' lanes. I’ve found that the [9]junction design[/9] is the most critical element in preventing accidents. If a cyclist feels exposed when they are turning, they simply won't use the route. We’ve been looking at 'Dutch-style' roundabouts which prioritize the safety of those on two wheels.

We also have to consider the 'last mile' problem—how people get from the train station to their final destination. I’ve noticed that the introduction of [10]electric-bike sharing[/10] schemes has completely changed the demographics of urban cyclists. We are seeing more elderly people and people in business suits cycling because the electric motor takes the effort out of hills and long distances. It’s making cycling accessible to everyone, not just the fit and young.

Data is our most powerful tool. We use sensors to track which routes are being used the most. I was surprised to find that [11]air quality[/11] was the primary concern for most potential cyclists. People are worried about breathing in exhaust fumes while they exercise. This has led us to design 'green corridors'—routes that take cyclists through parks and quiet backstreets rather than along main roads.

However, it’s not all about the roads. We also need to change the buildings where people work. I’ve found that providing [12]secure storage[/12] is more important to commuters than even having showers in the office. If someone is worried about their expensive bike being stolen, they will leave it at home and take the bus instead. We are now working with developers to ensure every new office block has a dedicated bike hub.

Looking forward, I’m excited about the concept of '15-minute cities', where everything you need is within a short ride of your home. I believe that [13]public perception[/13] is finally shifting. People are starting to see the bicycle as a symbol of a modern, healthy lifestyle rather than a nuisance on the road. It’s a slow process, but the momentum is definitely there.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]policy document[/14] for the Mayor’s office on the future of pedestrianized zones. We want to create more spaces where cars are banned entirely. I believe the future of our city is on two wheels, not four. Thank you for listening.
